<summary>Agent "Product review: does this plan produce the right outcome for readers?" completed</summary>
<result>I now have a thorough read of all three documents. Here is my structured product review.

---

# Product Review: Content Writing Pipeline Plan

## My honest overall read

The plan is technically rigorous and internally consistent. It is built by people who care about quality. It will not produce mediocre docs if fully executed. But it has a structural problem that is not about quality — it is about time-to-value. A first-time Orchestrator reader cannot benefit from any of this until Phase 6 completes, and the plan front-loads the most abstracted, least reader-facing work. The risk is not bad output. The risk is months of correct process before any reader gets any value.

---

## 1. Outcome Mapping — Does an Orchestrator reader get from "what is this?" to "running a node"?

If every phase completes in sequence: yes, eventually. But map it against the phase outputs and the gap is visible.

Phase 0 produces decisions-locked.md. Phase 1 is done and produced audience design. Phase 2 produces a content scan inventory. Phase 3 produces a tab-map with a work order. Phase 3.5 produces locked terminology. Phase 4 produces reconsolidation decisions. Phase 4.5 produces file moves and stubs. Phase 5 produces voice rule integration. At no point in Phases 0–5 does any reader receive a single written word.

Phase 6 is where the first sentence gets written. But Phase 6 itself is gated by all of the above, plus a hard dependency list of 6 items, plus an approved page brief per page before any write mode runs, plus a human approval on every write output before it moves to Phase 7. For a tab with ~536 pages (that number appears in the plan for Gateways), this is not a pipeline that ships content — it is a pipeline that processes content batch by batch, all approval-gated.

The journey from "what is this?" to "running a node" requires: a disambiguation page, an overview page, a prerequisites page, a node setup guide, and probably 3–5 operational reference pages. None of those exist as outputs until Phase 6 has run on them, Phase 8 has scored their headings, Phase 7 has cleared their claims, and Phase 10 has applied MDX. That is four passes plus multiple human approvals per page.

The gap: there is no fast path for the highest-value, most structurally complete pages. If the Orchestrator "getting started" section is already largely correct and just needs voice and minor veracity work, it goes through the same 12-phase pipeline as a stub that needs everything. The plan does not distinguish between pages that need rebuilding and pages that need light-touch improvement.

---

## 2. Reader-centricity — When does the reader's experience get designed?

Phase 1 is the closest thing to reader-centricity in the plan. It produced audience design docs covering personas, JTBD matrix, entry vectors, and journey maps. This is done for all 5 tabs. So the question "what does a Gateway operator arriving at the docs need to find, read, and do?" was designed in Phase 1.

But there are two problems.

First, Phase 1's output is a design document, not a page brief. The journey map exists as a collation output, but it does not automatically translate into: "therefore, the first page a Gateway operator sees should have this H1, this opening paragraph, and this primary action." That translation happens when a human writes a page brief (Phase 6 requirement) — but page briefs are written immediately before WRITE mode runs, after the entire audit and reconsolidation cycle. The reader-perspective design sits at the beginning of the pipeline but the actual application of that design is deferred to the end.

Second, Phase 1's output was called "done" but has open items that are structurally significant: the About multi-audience problem is unresolved, Delegators has a BLOCKING rewards placement decision, and the Orchestrators v5 learnings file is missing. Phase 1 is done enough to proceed but not done enough to trust as a locked input.

When is the reader experience actually designed? Technically Phase 1. Practically: when a human writes the first page brief in Phase 6 and has to decide what this page's opening line says. The plan does not surface this adequately. The page brief format in Phase 6 is: reader definition + one-sentence page outcome + prev/next page + single job + out-of-scope statement. That is a good format. But it comes after 5 phases of process and is written per page, not per journey. No one has designed the full Orchestrator journey as a sequence of concrete page briefs before Phase 6 starts writing. That means the first written pages may collectively produce a coherent journey or may not — you will find out when the critical review agent reads all outputs together at the end of a section.

This is the most significant reader-centricity gap: the journey-level design does not produce a pre-approved sequence of page briefs before content work starts. It produces a tab-map work order. A work order is not a reader journey.

---

## 3. Over-engineering risk — What could be collapsed or deferred?

The plan is aware of some of this: it already notes the prompt testing protocol was reduced from 3 agents to 1. That is the right instinct applied too narrowly.

What can be collapsed or deferred without losing quality:

Phase 9 (schema migration) is already confirmed as not blocking Phase 10. It is correctly called out as a parallel track. But it still appears in the main phase sequence as Phase 9, between Phase 8 and Phase 10, which misrepresents its relationship. Move it entirely out of the main sequence and into a separate track — it is tooling work, not content work.

The three parallel handoff packs (Pack 1, Pack 2, Pack 3) are a good structural idea. But Pack 3 (Phase 11 universal pages infrastructure) builds prompts for work that does not start until the first full tab completes Phase 10. Given that no tab has started Phase 2 yet, Pack 3 can be deferred for months without any effect. Building it now is premature investment. Flag it, defer it, do not build it until the first tab is 70% through Phase 6.

The Prompt Testing Protocol applied to every prompt before every production run adds overhead that is disproportionate to the risk for the simplest prompts. The naming rubric scoring prompt does not need 3 test runs and a learnings file before it produces heading scores. The content scan prompt probably does, because its output feeds everything downstream. The plan does not distinguish between high-leverage prompts (where test overhead is justified) and low-leverage prompts (where it is bureaucratic). Apply the full protocol to: content-scan.md, content-pass.md, veracity-pass.md. Apply a lighter version (1 test run, human eyeball, approve) to: naming rubric integration, glossary-finalizer, portal-navigator-generator.

The decision registry, blocking-items tracker, and tab-status board are all valuable. The gate definitions for each gate are also correct. But the blocking-items tracker is described as a runtime document populated by humans as they review outputs — which means it is empty until Phase 3 is running, which means there is no PM infrastructure until there is already content to manage. Create the registry and status board as immediate tasks (they are listed in the progress tracker). But do not let the PM infrastructure become a prerequisite for doing work — it should track work, not gate it.

The minimum viable pipeline to produce high-quality first-tab content: Phase 0 decisions locked, Phase 2 content scan, Phase 3 IA audit, Phase 3.5 terminology lock, Phase 6 content pass on the highest-priority section only (not full tab), Phase 8 naming, Phase 7 veracity, Phase 10 layout. That is 7 functional phases. The 5 additional phases (Phase 4, Phase 4.5, Phase 5 integration work, Phase 9, Phase 11, Phase 12) are either deferred-value, parallel tracks, or end-of-tab work. You could have published content from the first section of Gateways before Phase 4.5 reconsolidation even runs for other sections, if the pipeline allowed it.

---

## 4. Sequencing intuition — Is the ordering right?

The plan ordering is: Phase 0 → Phase 1 (done) → Phase 2 → Phase 3 → Phase 3.5 → Phase 4 → Phase 4.5 → Phase 5 → Phase 6 → Phase 8 → Phase 7 → Phase 9 → Phase 10 → Phase 11 → Phase 12.

There are two problems.

First problem: Phase 4 (content audit — reconsolidation decisions) and Phase 4.5 (file moves and stubs) precede Phase 6 (writing) for the entire tab. This means all IA restructuring must complete before any writing starts. For a large tab, this means months of structural work before a word is written. The alternative is section-by-section sequencing: complete Phase 4 for section 1, do Phase 4.5 for section 1, write section 1 in Phase 6, move to section 2. This is actually described partially in Phase 6 ("content work proceeds section by section") but Phase 4 is framed as a tab-level operation, not a section-level one. Reconcile this explicitly: Phase 4 and 4.5 should be section-gated, not tab-gated.

Second problem: Phase 5 (voice rules integration) is sandwiched between Phase 4.5 and Phase 6. But Phase 5 items — integrating the Brand & Copy Guide, adding persona-level differentiation, adding community routing table, fixing the false warning — are not dependencies of Phase 6. The false warning fix is a one-task correction. The Brand & Copy Guide integration is a one-document synthesis. These should be done immediately (they are listed in "What Can Be Built Immediately") rather than placed as a gated phase. The voice testing step in Phase 5 (3 pages per audience) is the only part that actually blocks Phase 6, because it validates whether the voice rules produce correct output before volume. Keep that. Remove the rest from the phase gate.

What would I change in sequence:

Do all the "Can Be Built Immediately" items first, in parallel, as an infrastructure sprint. This includes: content-scan.md, AUDIT mode, veracity-pass.md, decision-registry.md, tab-status.md, voice-rules.md fixes. This collapses about 3 months of sequential preparation into a 2-week parallel sprint.

Then run Phase 0 decisions and Phase 2 content scan simultaneously (one is human decisions, one is AI execution — they do not block each other).

Then run Phase 3 IA audit immediately after Phase 2, per tab.

Then run Phase 4 and Phase 6 in section-by-section alternation, not sequentially at tab level.

Defer Phase 11 prompts to when first tab is at Phase 8. Defer Phase 12 to when first two tabs complete Phase 10.

---

## 5. Bottlenecks — Where will this slow down?

Human review bottlenecks that will genuinely slow progress:

Phase 0 decisions. These are human-only strategic decisions about tab structure, rewards placement, S6 split, multi-audience model. They cannot be automated. And they block Phase 2 for the affected tabs. The plan correctly identifies these. But it does not set a deadline or urgency signal. If Phase 0 takes 3 weeks because decisions are scattered across conversations, everything else waits.

Page briefs. Every page requires an approved brief before WRITE mode runs in Phase 6. For a tab with 536 pages, this is 536 human approval events just to start writing. The plan does not acknowledge this as a bottleneck. It should. Either: (a) approve briefs in batches by section, (b) let AI pre-generate briefs from the approved tab-map and have the human scan-and-approve rather than write-and-approve, or (c) limit brief approval to the first 5 pages of a section and use section-level guidelines for the rest.

Human review of every WRITE output before Phase 7. Correct process. But if this means one page at a time, this is not a pipeline — it is a review queue. The plan allows parallel agents (one page per agent, multiple simultaneously within a section). The review should be structured similarly: review a section's output at once, not page by page.

Where AI can proceed further without human intervention: Phase 2 content scan (AI reads files, produces inventory, waits for human completeness check — human review is a quick completeness scan, not a judgment call). Phase 3 IA audit (gap analysis and work order are AI-produced; human is approving the priority classifications, not re-deriving them). Phase 8 naming scores (mechanical application of a rubric — human sees only headings that score below 20, not all headings). Phase 9 schema validators (pure engineering work — AI implements, human reviews test output). Phase 7 veracity (AI clears the 90% — human sees only the residual, no re-reading cleared claims).

Where human judgment is genuinely required: Phase 0 decisions (structural and strategic). Page brief approvals for the first pages of each new section type (to establish the pattern). Veracity residual items (the 10% that AI cannot resolve). Phase 4 reconsolidation decisions (MOVE/MERGE/DROP decisions have significant downstream consequences). Phase 12 cross-tab CTA placement (requires product judgment about graduation paths).

---

## 6. Missing content strategy — Who decides what the Gateway's disambiguation page says?

This is the sharpest gap in the plan.

The plan produces, by Phase 3, a tab-map that lists which pages exist, where they should go, what pageType they are, and what their P0/P1/P2 priority is. That is structural analysis. It does not contain a content strategy.

A content strategy would say: "The Gateway tab's entry point is the overview page. A Gateway operator arrives knowing they run infrastructure and wants to understand how Livepeer fits their existing setup. The H1 of the overview should immediately position Livepeer in terms of gateway economics and API compatibility, not in terms of the broader protocol. The opening paragraph answers: what will I be doing, and what does it cost me to get there. The second section is prerequisites..."

None of that is in the plan. The audience design output (Phase 1) has personas and JTBD — but JTBD is not a content strategy. "Maximize transcoding throughput while maintaining 99.9% uptime" is a job, not a page structure.

The page brief format (Phase 6) would capture this per page: one-sentence page outcome, single job this page does. But the briefs are written immediately before WRITE mode runs, after the IA audit. That means the person writing the brief is doing content strategy in real time, one page at a time, under the pressure of a write queue. There is no step that asks: before we start writing this section, here is what every page in this section is trying to accomplish, in what order, and how they hand off to each other.

This is a missing pre-Phase-6 step. I would call it a "section narrative design" step. For each approved section in the tab-map: what is the reader's state when they enter this section? what do they know when they leave? what is the emotional/practical tone of the opening page? This step produces a section-level brief that the page briefs inherit from, rather than each page brief being designed in isolation.

Without this step, the pipeline can produce individually correct pages that collectively feel fragmented. A reader arriving at the Orchestrators tab will experience each page as locally coherent but may not feel carried from one place to the next.

---

## 7. Feedback System — Is it acceptable to ship without it?

The Feedback System (Layer 6 of design-canonical.mdx) has no implementation phase in the plan. The plan's Gaps section notes this explicitly as Gap 8: "No formal process for corrections to feed back into standards. Define before Phase 6 produces significant output."

The plan acknowledges this. But placing it in a Gaps list at the bottom of the document is not the same as having a phase for it.

Here is what actually happens without a feedback system: Phase 6 writes 40 pages. Human reviewers correct voice in 8 of them. Those corrections exist in review comments, approval notes, or chat logs. The next 40 pages are written with the same voice rules as the first 40, because the corrections were never incorporated. The voice rules do not sharpen. The per-page approval rate does not improve. The human review load stays constant.

The design-canonical's ideal state for the Feedback System: "After 10 pages through the full pipeline, AI first-pass approval rate measurably higher than after page 1. Corrections per page trending down." The plan produces no mechanism for this to happen.

Is it acceptable to defer? In the short run, yes — shipping is the goal, not perfection. But the acceptable version of deferral is not "it is in the Gaps list." The acceptable version is: "When Phase 6 pilot runs for Gateways first section (approximately 40-60 pages), a human reviewing the output captures all corrections into a corrections log. After the section, those corrections are reviewed for patterns. Patterns update the relevant standards file. This is a lightweight feedback loop that runs at section boundary, not per page." That takes an afternoon to design and does not require building a system.

The plan should not ship phase 6 at scale without at minimum this lightweight version. If voice corrections accumulate without being captured, by the time the Developers tab is being written, the corrections from Gateways are inaccessible.

---

## Summary Assessment

The plan is structurally sound and technically correct. The risk is not quality — it is time-to-value and reader journey design.

The three things I would change before executing:

One: Run the "What Can Be Built Immediately" list as a two-week infrastructure sprint in parallel, treating all items as a single phase rather than a scattered list attached to individual phases. This pulls Phase 6 readiness significantly forward.

Two: Add a section narrative design step before Phase 6 for each section — not per page, but per section. This fills the gap between the JTBD in Phase 1 audience design and the page briefs in Phase 6. Ten minutes per section of asking "what is the reader's state entering and exiting this section?" prevents fragmentation that is very hard to fix after the fact.

Three: Define the lightweight feedback loop now, before Phase 6 runs. It does not need to be a system. It needs to be a habit: capture corrections at section boundary, review for patterns, update the relevant file. If this is not defined before Phase 6 starts, it will not happen at all.

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/Users/alisonhaire/.claude/plans/misty-rolling-starfish.md` - The execution plan being reviewed; the "What Can Be Built Immediately" section and the Gaps section are the most actionable parts
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/design-canonical.mdx` - The canonical system design; Layer 6 (Feedback System) is the most critically unimplemented layer
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/Prompts/Prompts-By-Phase/3-content-pass/content-pass.md` - Where page briefs and the WRITE mode live; the missing section narrative design step belongs here as a pre-flight addition
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/Prompts/Prompts-By-Phase/1-audience-design/testing/Tabs/gateways/collated/canonical-gateways-audience-design.md` - The Phase 1 output that must bridge into section-level narrative design; the gap between JTBD and page briefs lives here
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/decisions/decision-registry.md` - Does not yet exist; creating it is the single highest-leverage unblocking action in the entire plan</result>

---

## Strategic Assessment: misty-rolling-starfish vs. design-canonical

---

### 1. Canonical System Fidelity — 6-Layer Mapping

**The short answer**: The plan maps adequately to most layers, but Layer ① (Standards) and Layer ⑥ (Feedback) have alignment gaps. Layer ④ (Veracity) is correctly flagged but underweighted. The sequencing of the per-page pipeline layers (③ → ④ → ⑤) has a documented and defensible reordering — but one that introduces a risk the plan does not formally acknowledge.

**Layer by layer**:

**① Standards Layer** — Partial alignment, one structural gap

The design-canonical is explicit: the Standards Layer has three incomplete steps that block downstream work — Step 10 (page template structural contracts), Step 11 (frontmatter validator to 7-type schema), and the Standards Usage Guide. The plan correctly notes Steps 10 (as Phase 10 prep) and 11 (as Phase 9). But it does NOT carry forward the "Standards Usage Guide" (`_research-and-consolidated-notes/standards-guide.md`) — the canonical document that maps which definition file loads for which pipeline phase. This is described in design-canonical.mdx as the document that makes the standards self-administering.

The plan also flags a genuine gap the design-canonical does not: Brand & Copy Guide not integrated as base layer in voice-rules.md. This is a standards-layer item that the design-canonical did not anticipate. The plan handles it in Phase 5 but does not route it back to the Standards Layer as a formal output. It is treated as a Phase 5 task, not as a standards document change.

**② Context Layer** — Well-aligned

The plan's Phase 0 through Phase 4.5 correspond closely to the design-canonical ② steps: site-map, audience design, content scan, IA audit, content audit, reorganisation. The plan extends these correctly with parallel run paths, per-tab gates, and the Tab Status Board.

One gap: the design-canonical ② Audience Design accordion specifies that the IN for audience design includes the canonical `context-packs/site-map.md` (approved, at canonical path). The plan's Phase 0 notes the site map is not yet at canonical path and not yet human-approved. The plan correctly flags this as a prerequisite — but Phase 1 is marked ✅ Complete even though this upstream input was not in final approved state when Phase 1 ran. This is examined further under question 3.

**③ Content Engine** — Well-aligned structurally, one internal sequencing issue

The plan's Phase 6 correctly maps to ③. The AUDIT mode gap is correctly identified and tracked as P0. The plan goes further than the design-canonical by specifying the page brief format, per-page agent parallelism, and section-level review agents — this is good extension work.

The sequencing issue: the plan reorders the per-page pipeline to Phase 6 (content) → Phase 8 (naming) → Phase 7 (veracity) → Phase 10 (layout). This is architecturally sound — the rationale is that naming changes would orphan veracity markers if veracity runs before naming. This is a documented decision in the plan. However, the design-canonical's per-page pipeline is defined as ③ → ④ → ⑤ with no naming phase interleaved. The plan's Phase 8 (naming audit) is not explicitly a separate layer in the design-canonical; it falls inside ③ Content Engine's quality bar ("headings score ≥20/25"). By separating naming into its own phase between ③ and ④, the plan adds an unlabelled step in the pipeline that the design-canonical does not define. This is not wrong — it may be better — but it means the pipeline now has a phase that doesn't map to any canonical layer label, which will cause confusion when debugging or extending.

**④ Veracity Engine** — Correctly flagged as P0, but underweighted in urgency

The plan calls veracity-pass.md a P0 gap and lists it in "What Can Be Built Immediately." However, Phase 7 is given four paragraphs of spec detail. The design-canonical's ④ RESEARCH step (claim-type analysis: classify 20+ claims, map against source coverage, document gaps) is not carried forward into the plan at all. The plan jumps directly to building the prompt without the upstream research that the design-canonical says the prompt design depends on. The plan's veracity prompt spec (confidence scoring matrix, staleness matrix, auto-resolve rules) is well thought-out — but it is spec authored in a vacuum rather than derived from empirical claim-type analysis. This is the same pattern as the Standards Testing gap (design-canonical ① Testing accordion: all steps ❌, not formally run) — definitions and prompts are being built and used before the validation step that was supposed to precede them.

**⑤ Production Engine** — Correctly aligned

Phase 10 maps cleanly. The plan corrects a false blocker (schema migration already done) and correctly identifies the remaining prep: template contracts, component profile reconciliation, golden examples. The layout-pass prompt exists. The plan is explicit that Phase 10 is blocked by upstream phases 6+7+8, not by schema — this is a material correction from the design-canonical's stated status.

**⑥ Feedback System** — Absent from the plan

The design-canonical ⑥ is fully defined: correction-to-file routing map, feedback log, correction routing reference, periodic review process. All four outputs are ❌ in the design-canonical. The plan does not address any of them. Layer ⑥ does not appear in the 12 phases, in the "What Can Be Built Immediately" table, or in the PM infrastructure section. The plan describes a system that produces content but has no mechanism to improve from corrections. Given that the design-canonical's quality bar for ⑥ is explicitly "after 10 pages through the full pipeline, AI first-pass approval rate measurably higher than after page 1," this is a missing architectural layer, not a deferred feature. Without ⑥, every tab after Gateways runs the same quality level as page 1 of Gateways.

---

### 2. The design-canonical → plan Relationship

The plan's own header states its relationship to the design-canonical correctly: "takes Steps from design-canonical accordions and extends them with parallel run paths, handoffs, phase sequencing, and a decision log."

In practice, this is partly true and partly not. Assessment by component:

**Where extension is happening correctly**: Phase 0 (gate system, decision registry), the Tab Status Board, the parallel agent structure, the per-tab handoff packs, the Prompt Testing Protocol, and the detailed Phase 2/3/4 specs. These are genuine extensions — the design-canonical defines WHAT; the plan defines HOW and in what order.

**Where the plan diverges rather than extends**: The plan adds Phase 3.5 (Terminology & Naming Pre-Work) and Phase 8 (Naming Audit) as independent phases. Neither maps to a named layer in the design-canonical. Phase 3.5 exists because the plan discovered that terminology decisions need a dedicated lock phase — this is real and correct, but it represents execution knowledge that was not fed back into the design-canonical. The design-canonical does not know this phase exists. If someone reads the design-canonical to understand the system, they will have an incomplete picture.

**Where the plan silently drops design-canonical requirements**: ① Standards Testing (❌ not formally run in design-canonical), ① HUMAN REVIEW lock (informally done, not formally stamped), ① Standards Usage Guide (❌ not built), ⑥ Feedback System (entirely absent from the plan). These are not extensions — they are omissions. The plan does not say "⑥ is deferred because X" — it simply does not mention it.

**Verdict**: The plan is extending the design-canonical for Layers ② through ⑤ reasonably well. It is not extending ① and has dropped ⑥ entirely. This means the system as described in the plan is not the system as specified in the design-canonical.

---

### 3. Phase 1 (Audience Design) — Is "Complete" Accurate?

The plan marks Phase 1 ✅ Complete with outstanding items. The design-canonical ② Audience Design step defines what "complete" means:

- `context-packs/[tab]-audience-doc.md` must exist (approved)
- Step 3 of the design-canonical: "Run for first tab (pilot: Orchestrators)" — ❌ not run
- Step 4: "Human review + approval of audience doc" — ❌ not done

The plan's version is different: "v5 runs + canonical collation outputs exist for all 5 priority tabs." The plan is saying the prompts ran and produced output. The design-canonical is saying the output must be reviewed and approved before proceeding.

Specifically: the design-canonical's IN for Audience Design requires `context-packs/site-map.md` (approved, at canonical path). The plan acknowledges the site map is "not yet at canonical path" and "human approval pending." This means Phase 1 was run with an input that was not in final approved state. Outputs built on an unapproved input are themselves unapproved.

The plan partially acknowledges this: "Outstanding items (must resolve at Phase 0)" lists Delegators rewards placement, Developers S6 split, About multi-audience model, and unresolved BLOCKING terminology items. But these are framed as items to resolve at Phase 0, not as items that make Phase 1's outputs conditional. The distinction matters: if the Delegators rewards placement decision changes the section structure, the audience design output for Delegators may need to be revised.

**Verdict**: Phase 1 is more accurately described as "Draft outputs exist for all 5 tabs · Pending human approval and Phase 0 decisions." Calling it Complete conflates "ran" with "approved." The design-canonical's exit condition for audience design requires human review and approval, neither of which has formally occurred.

---

### 4. Framework Files — Completeness and Lock Status

The plan lists these as complete: pageType, pagePurpose, information-type, veracity, veracity-library, complexity, industry, voice-rules, section-naming, 08a-ia-per-tab.

**Actual status based on files in the repo**:

- `veracity.md` — explicitly marked `Status: DRAFT` in the file header. The plan calls it "exists (draft, not yet locked)." The design-canonical requires it to be locked before the veracity pass prompt can be built. The plan builds the veracity pass spec directly from a DRAFT framework file. This means the prompt could be built against definitions that change when veracity.md is locked.

- `section-naming.md` rubric — the plan explicitly notes it has "NOT been tested or reviewed in production" and "locking means the structure is finalised, not that quality is validated." The design-canonical ① Testing step (validate definitions produce correct output on real pages) is marked ❌ not formally run for this file. The plan is about to use this rubric at scale in Phase 8 having never validated that AI classification matches human judgment on real pages.

- `content-pipeline-framework.md` — referenced throughout as "canonical schema — 7 types, 15 purposes, frontmatter spec" but not listed in the design-canonical outputs table. It appears to be a synthesised reference doc. Its authority relative to the Frameworks/ files needs to be clarified — it is referenced everywhere in agent context packs but its lock status is undefined.

- `prompt-input-spec.md` — present in `Frameworks/` (found by glob). This corresponds to the design-canonical ① Step 8 (Define prompt input spec) which is listed as ❌ Not started in plan-canonical.md. If this file exists, the design-canonical's status is wrong. The plan does not reference this file anywhere in Phase 6 or the agent context packs.

**The governance gap**: The design-canonical ① HUMAN REVIEW accordion states each definition file must be "marked locked with version date" and MASTER-INDEX.md updated. The plan acknowledges this is "informally locked, no formal version/date stamping." Without version dates, it is impossible to determine whether a definition a prompt is using was the version that was approved or a subsequent draft.

---

### 5. Prompt Architecture — Pipeline Coherence

The plan defines five prompts forming a pipeline: audience-design → structure-audit → content-scan → content-pass (WRITE/AUDIT) → veracity-pass → layout-pass.

**Input/output compatibility checks needed**:

The plan correctly requires that `content-scan.md` output must be validated against `structure-audit.md` input requirements before going to production (Pack 1 handoff specifies this). This is good engineering discipline.

However, the same check is not explicitly required for content-pass → veracity-pass and veracity-pass → layout-pass handoffs. The veracity-pass prompt does not yet exist; when it is built, the plan does not specify that its output format must be validated against layout-pass.md input requirements. The `{/* REVIEW: [claim] — verify with: [source] */}` flag format is defined in the plan, but the layout-pass prompt's handling of these markers (whether it strips them, preserves them, or errors on them) is not specified.

**The Phase 8 insertion creates an unlabelled handoff**: The pipeline is now content-pass output → naming audit input → naming audit output → veracity-pass input. The naming audit output format is not defined in the plan. What does a "named" page look like structurally vs the content-pass output? Are all headings revised in place? Is there a log of changes? The design-canonical has no guidance here because it does not define Phase 8 as a separate pipeline step.

**The AUDIT mode gap is a pipeline break, not just a missing feature**: AUDIT mode's output (`context-packs/[tab]/[group]-audit.md`) is the input to Phase 6 WRITE runs via approved page briefs. Without AUDIT mode, the Phase 6 process described in the plan cannot start — there are no approved briefs to write from. The plan correctly tracks this as P0, but the dependency chain makes it more critical than a simple "missing prompt": it is the gate that converts Phase 4 outputs into Phase 6 inputs.

---

### 6. Standards Governance — PM Section vs. Design-Canonical

The design-canonical ① Standards Layer governs how design decisions are made and enforced. It requires:
- Every definition independently produces correct AI output before being locked
- No ambiguity between files
- A Standards Usage Guide mapping which definition loads for which phase
- Formal version/date stamping per locked file

The plan's PM section introduces:
- Decision Registry — decisions used as dependencies must be in the registry
- Tab Status Board — per-tab gate tracking
- Blocking Item Tracker — populated by humans from agent outputs
- Gate Definitions — artefact + sign-off required per gate

**What the plan has invented that the design-canonical does not define**: The Tab Status Board, Gate Definitions, and Blocking Item Tracker are execution infrastructure the design-canonical never specified. These are appropriate additions for running a multi-tab operation — the design-canonical's Standards Layer was not designed with parallel tab execution in mind.

**What the plan omits that the design-canonical requires**: The plan's Decision Registry governs execution decisions (terminology, structure, schema). The design-canonical's Standards Layer governs definition quality — specifically the TESTING and HUMAN REVIEW accordions. The plan has no equivalent mechanism: there is no registry of which definition files have passed AI classification testing, at what accuracy rate, and when they were locked. Without this, the "locked" label on a definition file is informational, not governed.

The plan and the design-canonical are running parallel governance systems for different layers:
- Design-canonical governs: definition quality and accuracy
- Plan governs: execution decisions and tab progress

Neither system covers the other's domain. The gap is: who governs whether a definition file is actually correct before it is used at scale?

---

### 7. Exit Criteria — What "Done" Looks Like Per Phase

The design-canonical defines explicit "DONE WHEN" criteria for the phases that have them:
- ① Testing: AI classification matches human judgment on ≥4 of 5 test pages per definition
- ③ Testing: Average ≤1 revision cycle per page before content approval
- ④ Testing: ≥90% clearance rate, residuals all actionable, false positive rate <5%
- ⑤ Testing: All test pages render clean, heading scores ≥20/25, no old-schema values

The plan's Phase Gate conditions are:
- Phase 1 Gate: checklist of steps (Steps 1–7, 8a, 9, 10 complete; Steps 8 and 11 outstanding)
- Phase 2 Gate: checklist of steps (prompts tested, schema migrated, P0 gaps resolved)
- Phase 3 Gate: metrics-based (precision ≥90%, recall ≥80%, human acceptance ≥80%)

**Alignment assessment**: Phase 3 gate (the plan's Phase 3 = design-canonical's ③ testing target) is correctly numeric and matches the spirit of the design-canonical quality bars. But Phases 1 and 2 gates are step-completion checklists, not quality criteria. Phase 2 says "all prompts tested" without specifying what passing a test looks like — this maps loosely to the design-canonical's testing accordions but does not inherit the specific numeric criteria.

**The larger issue**: The design-canonical defines done-when at the layer level (per system). The plan defines done-when at the tab level (per gate). These are compatible but not the same. A tab can pass all gate conditions while a layer-level quality bar is still failing. For example: a tab could have an "approved" content scan (Tab Status Board: Content Scan Done = ✅) while the content-scan.md prompt has never passed the ③ Testing criterion of ≤1 revision cycle per page. The tab gate and the layer gate are tracking different things.

---

### Summary: Alignment vs. Gap

**Well-aligned**:
- Phase structure for Layers ②–⑤ (Context through Production): phases map, extensions are appropriate
- P0 gap identification: content-scan, AUDIT mode, veracity-pass are all correctly flagged
- Corrected false statuses: frontmatter migration, voice rules coverage, Phase 1 run status
- Multi-tab parallelism: the Tab Status Board and per-tab gate system is a clean addition
- Prompt Testing Protocol: a real improvement on what the design-canonical specified
- Decision Registry concept: the right governance mechanism for execution decisions
- Phase 7 reordering (naming before veracity): documented, defensible, correct

**Misaligned or missing**:

1. **Layer ⑥ absent**: The Feedback System is the mechanism that makes the pipeline improve over time. It is not in the plan, not deferred with explanation, not mentioned. This is not a minor omission — it means the plan produces content but the system does not learn.

2. **Phase 1 marked Complete incorrectly**: v5 runs exist; approved outputs do not. The design-canonical exit condition for audience design requires human review and approval. Neither has formally occurred. "Complete" should be "Draft runs complete, approval pending."

3. **veracity.md is DRAFT, not locked**: The plan builds the veracity pass spec against an unlocked framework. The design-canonical's ① Testing step (which validates definitions before use) has not been run for any definition file. Using definitions at scale without this validation is the architectural risk the design-canonical was built to prevent.

4. **Standards Usage Guide not carried forward**: The design-canonical ① documents step — mapping which definition loads for which phase — is ❌ not built. The plan does not inherit this item. Without it, the standards are not self-administering.

5. **No layer-level quality bars in phase gates**: Phase gates are step-completion checklists. The design-canonical's DONE WHEN criteria (numeric thresholds per layer) are not integrated into the gate conditions. A tab can be "open" for Phase 6 while the content-pass prompt has never demonstrated ≤1 revision cycle performance.

6. **Phase 8 creates an unlabelled pipeline step**: The plan correctly identifies that naming must precede veracity. The design-canonical does not define Phase 8 as a separate layer. The output format of a naming audit and its handoff to the veracity pass are undefined. This will become a concrete problem when the pipeline runs.

7. **prompt-input-spec.md appears to exist but is not referenced**: A file matching the design-canonical ① Step 8 scope exists at `/Frameworks/prompt-input-spec.md` but the plan lists this step as ❌ Not started and does not include it in any agent context pack. Either the file is incomplete or the plan has a stale status.

---

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/design-canonical.mdx` - The canonical system definition; ⑥ Feedback System outputs and ① Testing/HUMAN REVIEW criteria must be explicitly inherited into the plan
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/Frameworks/veracity.md` - Status: DRAFT; must be locked before veracity-pass.md prompt spec is finalised
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/Frameworks/prompt-input-spec.md` - May already contain Step 8 output; status and contents need reconciliation with plan
- `/Users/alisonhaire/.claude/plans/misty-rolling-starfish.md` - The plan under review; specific sections requiring correction: Phase 1 status, Phase 7 veracity.md dependency, absence of ⑥ layer
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/plan-canonical.md` - The execution plan this extends; Phase Gate conditions need numeric quality bars added to match design-canonical DONE WHEN criteria</result>

---

## Adversarial Review: Content Writing Pipeline Plan

---

### 1. The "Good Enough" Trap

**Is this a real risk?** Yes, and it is the highest-probability failure mode in this plan.

**How severe?** Severe. The plan has multiple structural safeguards — gate definitions with specific artefact requirements, explicit human sign-off per gate, blocking-items.md populated by humans not AI — but none of these prevent a human from signing off on a gate quickly. The tab-status board tracks whether a gate is checked, not whether the check was rigorous. An approved checkbox is identical whether it took 20 minutes or 20 seconds.

The plan's Phase 3 tab-map approval is the most vulnerable gate. It is a long, complex document (IA map, gap analysis, P0/P1/P2 prioritisation) reviewed once before all of Phase 6 runs. A rubber-stamped tab-map doesn't just miss a few pages — it propagates errors through every brief, every written page, every review in Phase 6. The downstream correction cost is high because Phase 6 agents are briefed from the tab-map.

Phase 6 briefs are the second highest risk. The plan requires "human approves every page brief before writing begins." For a tab with ~40-60 pages in a pilot section alone, that is 40-60 brief reviews before writing even starts. At genuine quality, this requires substantial time per brief. Under time pressure, the review collapses to "looks reasonable."

**Minimum mitigation that would actually help:**

The plan needs to distinguish between two types of approval: structural approvals (tab-map, reconsolidation plan) and volume approvals (per-page briefs, per-page outputs). Structural approvals must be thorough — they are one-time decisions with wide downstream impact. Volume approvals need a sampling protocol rather than 100% review.

Specifically: for the tab-map gate, require that the human reviewer explicitly answers three questions before signing off: (1) Does this IA match the canonical audience design for positions 1-3? (2) Are all P0 gaps correctly identified? (3) Is the first section's page list complete and in the right order? These questions have to be answered in writing, in the gate sign-off document. A checkbox without written answers is not a gate.

For per-page briefs in Phase 6, replace 100% review with a batch sampling approach: human reviews 100% of briefs for the first section, then a random 25% sample for subsequent sections, with a mandatory escalation if any sampled brief is rejected. This actually preserves quality signal without requiring review of every brief.

The plan as written does not distinguish these two cases — all gates are treated as equal-weight checkboxes, which means everything drifts toward the approval cadence that is sustainable under time pressure, which is rubber stamps.

---

### 2. Context Decay

**Is this a real risk?** Yes, and it is specifically real for this plan, not just in general.

**How severe?** High for the structural design decisions; moderate for voice and terminology because those have good mitigation already.

The plan has two categories of context that need to survive from Phase 1 to Phase 6:

The first is terminology and voice, which the plan handles reasonably well. Terminology lock in Phase 3.5, voice rules loaded as explicit files in every content agent context pack, terminology decisions log — these collectively mean a Phase 6 agent will have the right vocabulary even without Phase 1 context.

The second is reader design intent — why sections are structured the way they are, what jobs the reader is trying to do, what the reader already knows when they arrive at each section. This is in the canonical audience design document, which is in the Phase 6 context pack. But "in the context pack" is not the same as "holding up under 50+ page documents of upstream design." The canonical audience design output for a tab is a dense document. When a Phase 6 agent is given a context pack that also includes tab-map, voice rules, naming rubric, copy rules, component registry, and the specific page file, the audience design inevitably gets the least attention.

The specific failure mode is: a Phase 6 agent writes technically correct content (right terminology, right voice register, right heading scores) that fails to serve the reader's actual job. This is hard to catch because the output looks correct at every formal check. The review agent in Phase 6 checks "journey coherence, voice consistency, terminology consistency" — but not "does this page actually answer the question a gateway operator has at this point in their journey?"

**Minimum mitigation that would actually help:**

The plan needs a condensed "reader design brief" — not the full 40-page audience design document, but a one-page document per tab that contains: the three primary reader jobs in ranked order, the arriving knowledge assumption (what the reader knows when they start), the two questions a reader should be able to answer after completing this section, and what they do next. This brief should be the first item in every Phase 6 context pack, not buried after five other files.

The phase-0-decisions-lock.md is a partial version of this — but it is a decision log, not a reader design brief. Reader design briefs need to be written specifically for Phase 6 consumption: short, actionable, in the reader's terms.

---

### 3. Decision Archaeology

**Is this a real risk?** Yes, and the recovery cost is asymmetric depending on which type of decision turns out to be wrong.

**How severe?** The plan does identify this as a structural concern — the decisions table in the header (rewards placement, S6 split, About multi-audience model) correctly flags these as blocking Phase 6. The decision-registry.md architecture is sound for the recovery path in principle.

However, the plan does not model the actual cost of structural decision reversal at Phase 8. Consider: Rewards placement (before or after operator selection) shapes the IA map for the Delegators tab (Phase 3). Every Phase 4 audit decision is made against that IA. Every Phase 6 brief is written against that IA. If the rewards placement decision is reversed at Phase 8, it is not "update the plan" — it is rebuild the tab-map, redo the audit decisions for two section groups, rewrite the affected page briefs, and rewrite the pages that reference "before you set up rewards, first do X." The effort is roughly equivalent to Phase 3 + Phase 4 + Phase 4.5 + affected Phase 6 pages.

The plan does correctly make rewards placement a Phase 0 BLOCKING decision — so theoretically this shouldn't happen. But the adversarial scenario is: what if Phase 0 gets a "provisional" approval that turns out to be wrong once real content is being written? Or what if the Delegators tab is at Phase 4 and the product team changes the UX flow that the rewards placement depends on?

**Minimum mitigation that would actually help:**

Two things. First, the decision-registry.md should record not just what was decided but what evidence would force a reversal. For each structural decision, one line: "This decision holds as long as: [condition]." If that condition is violated by a product change, the plan knows immediately which phases need to be rebuilt, rather than discovering it mid-Phase 6.

Second, the plan should sequence the three BLOCKING structural decisions (rewards placement, S6 split, About multi-audience model) as genuinely first before any tab-map work for those tabs — not just "BLOCKING for Phase 6" as currently stated, but "tab cannot enter Phase 3 without this decision logged." The current plan says "BLOCKING for Phase 6" which technically permits Phase 3 to run before the decision is made. Phase 3 produces an IA map that encodes the structural assumptions about rewards placement. That is the actual point of no cheap return.

---

### 4. Agent Consistency Failure

**Is this a real risk?** Yes. This is the risk most likely to produce a reader experience that is technically approved but actually bad.

**How severe?** High. The plan has reasonable mitigation infrastructure: voice rules, terminology lock, naming rubric, section review agent checking "voice consistency" across a section. But these controls operate at the sentence-structure and word-choice level. They do not prevent the more subtle consistency problems that emerge when 20+ agents each make independent micro-decisions about paragraph density, when to use a code example vs prose explanation, how much to hedge technical claims, how long introductions should be, when to use headers vs run-on paragraphs.

The per-section review agent is the right instinct — one agent reads all pages in a section together and checks coherence. But the review agent's mandate as stated is: "journey coherence (prev → this → next handoffs), voice consistency, terminology consistency." This is a completeness check, not a character check. It will catch obvious divergences (a page that uses wrong terminology or clearly wrong register) but not the accumulated micro-inconsistency that makes a reader feel they are jumping between different authors.

The most likely real failure: the first section of Gateways (the pilot) will have good consistency because fewer agents write fewer pages and there is more human attention per page. Later sections and later tabs will have increasing drift because the review cycles are faster and humans are approving more output with less scrutiny. By the time the Developers tab Phase 6 runs, the baseline expectations are set by Gateways — but the agents writing Developers pages have not read Gateways pages.

**Minimum mitigation that would actually help:**

The plan needs golden examples and they need to be in every content agent context pack, not as a Phase 10 prep task. Currently, golden examples are in "Phase 10 remaining prep" — this is too late. A Phase 6 agent writing the third page in the Gateways guides section should have a golden example of what a completed, approved page in that section looks like. Not a template — an actual finished example showing paragraph density, heading style, code example placement, prose length.

The golden example can be a single page from early in the pilot (once the first section is approved and meets quality). Lock that page, add it to the context pack. This is concrete and costs nothing once the first section completes.

Additionally, the section review agent's mandate should explicitly include: "Is this page consistent with the other pages in this section in terms of paragraph density and prose length?" These are not covered by the current voice/terminology checks.

---

### 5. Human Bottleneck Reality

**Is this a real risk?** Yes. This is the practical constraint that determines whether the plan completes.

**How severe?** This is the actual pacing constraint, not a quality risk per se, but it determines whether quality controls hold or get abandoned.

Let me count the actual approvals for one tab through Phase 6:

Phase 0: 1 structural approval (tab structure + audience design). Phase 3: 1 tab-map approval (but the map covers all sections, so this is a large document). Phase 3.5: 1 terminology lock. Phase 4: 1 reconsolidation plan approval per section group. If a tab has 5 section groups, that is 5 approvals at Phase 4. Phase 4.5: 1 docs.json review per section group = 5 more. Phase 6 briefs: ~40-60 pages for the pilot section alone, each requires a brief approval before writing. Phase 6 output: ~40-60 page-output approvals before moving to Phase 8. Phase 8: naming audit review.

So for Gateways alone, Phase 6 requires approximately 80-120 human review actions (40-60 brief approvals + 40-60 output approvals) for the first section. The plan describes the full tab as 536 pages. Even with parallel agents handling multiple pages, the human review serializes it.

This is not a design flaw — it is an intentional choice to maintain human quality control. But the plan does not acknowledge or model the time cost, which means there is no decision point for "should we run this differently for sections 4 and 5 than we did for sections 1 and 2?"

**Minimum mitigation that would actually help:**

The plan needs an honest review capacity model before Phase 6 starts. Specifically: estimate how many pages a human can genuinely review per day (brief + output), and whether that person is available for that duration. Then decide: do we front-load quality controls (100% review for Gateways, sampling for later tabs) or do we redesign the brief approval process?

The minimum viable change: after the first section of the pilot, move from "approve every brief individually" to "approve the brief template + section spec, then sampling." The brief template + section spec defines what every brief in a section should say. An agent that follows the template does not need individual brief review for every page — review 4-5 briefs to confirm compliance with the template, then write.

This is a real design change, not process theatre, and it requires saying explicitly in the plan: "brief review is 100% for Section 1, sampling thereafter, with escalation protocol."

---

### 6. Veracity Pass Timing Problem

**Is this a real risk?** Yes. The plan has already corrected the major ordering problem (Phase 8 naming before Phase 7 veracity). But there is a residual issue.

**How severe?** Moderate. The plan's correction is: Phase 6 → Phase 8 (naming) → Phase 7 (veracity). This is right. But the residual problem is intra-Phase 8: naming audit changes headings, but if the Phase 7 veracity pass runs after Phase 8 completes, it is operating on the renamed content. So far so good. The problem is when a veracity REVIEW marker says "This claim about [section name] needs verification" — and then Phase 8 renames that section. The marker still references the old section name in its text, even though it now sits in renamed content.

This is not catastrophic — the REVIEW markers are positioned immediately before the flagged block, not by section name reference. They are inline markers that point to specific content, not section anchors. So a Phase 8 rename does not orphan the marker location. The marker will still be above the same content block.

The genuine residual risk is more subtle: if Phase 8 naming changes cause content reorganisation (a section split that moves content between pages, not just heading renaming), then a veracity marker placed on a content block in Page A might end up in Page B after reorganisation. Phase 8 is supposed to be "naming only" — but if the naming rubric scores reveal structural problems (wrong heading hierarchy, a section that should be split), Phase 8 might trigger small restructuring decisions.

**Minimum mitigation that would actually help:**

The plan should make the Phase 8 scope boundary explicit: Phase 8 changes section titles only — it does not reorganise content between pages. Any Phase 8 finding that would require moving content between pages is flagged for human decision but deferred to a Phase 4.5 equivalent (already existing in the plan). This keeps the Phase 8 → Phase 7 ordering clean.

This boundary is implicit in the plan already but not stated as an explicit constraint. Adding it removes the residual ambiguity.

---

### 7. AI-Generated vs Human-Curated Content

**Is this a real risk?** Yes, and it is more subtle than it looks.

**How severe?** This is a long-term quality risk, not an immediate execution risk. The plan correctly identifies `resources/glossary.mdx` as human-made and authoritative. The risk is not that the AI will corrupt the glossary — the plan has clear glossary authority rules. The risk is the comparison problem: a reader who uses the human-curated glossary, then reads AI-generated pages, will notice a quality gap they cannot name. Human-authored documentation has a specificity and editorial economy that AI content typically lacks even when it is technically correct. Human pages tend to have exactly the right level of detail per claim; AI pages tend to be either over-explained (because the agent fills context it wasn't told the reader already has) or under-specified (because the agent follows templates and misses the place where a real expert would add the note that matters).

The review agent in Phase 6 checks voice consistency and journey coherence, but it does not check whether a page achieves the specificity level of the human-authored content that will sit next to it.

**Minimum mitigation that would actually help:**

The golden examples (Phase 10 prep) need to include at least one human-authored page as a quality benchmark, not just a structural template. When Phase 6 agents are given context, they should see "here is an approved human-written page from this repo — this is the quality target." This does not guarantee AI pages reach that level, but it gives the review agent a concrete calibration point.

Additionally, the Phase 6 review agent should be explicitly given this question: "Does this page demonstrate the specificity level and editorial economy of the human-authored reference examples?" Without that question in its mandate, the review agent cannot flag over-explanation or missing key details.

This matters most for the technical tabs (Developers, Orchestrators) where a reader who is actually competent in the domain will notice thin treatment of a subject even when it is technically accurate.

---

### 8. Scope Creep on "Complete" / Phase 12 Incremental vs. Wait

**Is this a real risk?** Yes. This is a structural dependency problem that the plan does not resolve.

**How severe?** Moderate in isolation; potentially blocking in practice.

The plan states: "Cross-tab phases (Phase 12) require all tabs to have completed their upstream phases." The execution order table shows Gateways at priority 1, Delegators at priority 5 with a BLOCKING decision that has not yet been made. The realistic scenario: Gateways finishes Phase 10 while Delegators is still in Phase 3 waiting for the rewards placement decision. Phase 12 cannot run. The wait could be weeks.

The cross-tab-journeys.md stub is supposed to be created during Phase 3 — this is smart and partially addresses it. It means Phase 12's inputs are being built incrementally throughout the process, not just at the end. But the actual consistency check (spot-check 20 key terms across tabs, verify cross-tab CTAs, etc.) requires content to exist in all 5 tabs.

The specific failure mode: if Phase 12 is run incrementally (checking Gateways + Orchestrators + Developers before Delegators and About complete), and then Delegators completes with terminology choices that conflict with what was locked in Phase 12 for the other 3 tabs, Phase 12 has to be re-run. This is not catastrophic but it is duplicate work.

There is also a scope creep risk in the opposite direction: if teams wait for all 5 tabs to reach Phase 10 before starting Phase 12, they may decide to "just ship" tabs that are ready rather than waiting, at which point Phase 12 never runs or runs as a post-launch cleanup.

**Minimum mitigation that would actually help:**

The plan needs a Phase 12 trigger decision recorded in Phase 0: do we run Phase 12 once (all tabs complete) or incrementally (as groups of tabs complete)? If incrementally, define the minimum tab set for a first Phase 12 run (suggestion: any 3 tabs that share a cross-tab journey, e.g. Gateways + Orchestrators + Developers share a graduation path). Document that an incremental Phase 12 run is provisional — it locks cross-tab conventions for the tabs that ran, but leaves open "pending Delegators and About" items.

The cross-tab-journeys.md stub should include this Phase 12 trigger decision as a field, created at Phase 3, so that by the time the first tab reaches Phase 10 there is already a logged decision on how Phase 12 will be triggered.

---

## Summary: Which Risks Are Acceptable vs Which Would Produce Bad Outcomes for Readers

**Would produce bad outcomes for readers if not mitigated:**

Risk 4 (agent consistency) and Risk 7 (AI vs human quality gap) are the ones that produce a bad reader experience. They are hard to detect through the plan's own quality controls because they are not compliance failures — they are quality failures. The plan's review mechanisms check completeness and correctness, not voice character and specificity depth. Readers will notice before reviewers do.

**Would produce operational failure / project failure if not mitigated:**

Risk 5 (human bottleneck) is the practical constraint. If the plan runs without an explicit review capacity model and sampling protocol, it will either stall at Phase 6 (reviewers overwhelmed) or degrade at Phase 6 (reviewers rubber-stamping). Either outcome means the plan's quality architecture doesn't actually run as designed.

Risk 3 (decision archaeology / structural decisions in Phase 0) is the recovery cost risk. If Rewards placement or S6 split decisions are made provisionally and then reversed after Phase 3 runs, the rework is substantial. The fix (make these genuinely blocking before Phase 3, not just before Phase 6) is low-cost to implement now.

**Acceptable / manageable with current mitigations:**

Risk 1 (good enough trap) — the gate architecture is imperfect but better than typical. If structural approvals require written answers to specific questions (not just checkboxes), the risk is manageable.

Risk 2 (context decay) — the plan's terminology lock and voice rules are good mitigation. The reader design brief gap is real but fixable by condensing Phase 1 outputs into a one-page brief format.

Risk 6 (veracity timing) — already partially addressed by the plan's Phase 8-before-Phase-7 correction. The residual scope boundary clarification is minor.

Risk 8 (Phase 12 incrementalism) — manageable with an explicit trigger decision in Phase 0. Not a quality risk, an operational coordination risk.

### Critical Files for Implementation

- `/Users/alisonhaire/.claude/plans/misty-rolling-starfish.md` - The plan itself; Phase 6 and the gate definitions are where the highest-risk sections require structural changes
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/decisions/decision-registry.md` - Does not yet exist; once created, the "reversal conditions" addition to each structural decision is the mitigation for Risk 3
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/Prompts/Prompts-By-Phase/3-content-pass/content-pass.md` - Phase 6 review agent mandate needs the specificity check and golden example reference to mitigate Risks 4 and 7
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/decisions/tab-status.md` - Does not yet exist; the gate sign-off format needs written answers, not checkboxes, to prevent Risk 1 from collapsing gates into theatre
- `/Users/alisonhaire/Documents/Livepeer/Docs-v2-dev/workspace/plan/active/CONTENT-WRITING/decisions/cross-tab-journeys.md` - Does not yet exist; needs Phase 12 trigger decision added as a field at creation time to prevent Risk 8 from becoming a late-stage coordination problem</result>

### Task 1 - Superseded prompt versions at `1-audience-design/` root (5 files)

<summary>Agent "Product review: canonical system alignment" completed</summary>
<result>I now have a complete picture of all three documents. Here is the assessment.

---

## 1. Canonical Alignment Verdict

The plan partially executes the canonical system design but has a structural distortion: it compresses Layers 1 and 2 of the canonical system into "pre-work" that is treated as effectively done, then sequences Layers 3–5 across Phases 6–10 correctly. The major gap is that the plan acknowledges all six canonical layers exist but does not build Layer 6 (Feedback System) as an executable system — it is named as a gap (item 8 in the Gaps Flagged section) and deferred with no phase assigned to build it. Layer 4 (Veracity Engine) is a P0 gap with no delivery phase — Phase 7 says "build it" but carries only a spec, no prompt, no test plan, no skill wrapper, and no pack guide. The plan is therefore not a complete execution of the canonical system; it is a plan for Layers 1–5 with Layer 6 intentionally unbuilt and Layer 4 still at the spec stage.

---

## 2. Weakest Layer

**Layer 6 — Feedback System** is the weakest execution in this plan. The canonical design defines a closed-loop system where corrections from Layers 3, 4, and 5 route back to Layer 1 (Standards) and Layer 2 (Context), preventing repeated corrections for the same class of issue. The plan lists this as gap item 8 with the note "define before Phase 6 produces significant output" — but assigns no phase to build it, no responsible agent type, no output artefact path, no pack guide, and no human gate. This means the plan builds a system that produces pages but has no mechanism for the pages' corrections to improve future pages. A note that something should be defined is not the same as a phase that builds it.

The three canonical Feedback System artefacts (correction-to-file routing map, feedback-log.md, correction-routing.md) are all marked ❌ in design-canonical.mdx with no plan phase targeting any of them. The plan effectively defers the Feedback System indefinitely while planning to run Phase 6 at production volume across five tabs.

---

## 3. Feedback Loop Assessment

This is a one-pass batch system with a deferred feedback acknowledgment. There is no real mechanism for corrections to propagate back into standards in the current plan. The closest approximation is the Decision Registry (`decision-registry.md`) and Terminology Decisions log (`terminology-decisions.md`) — these capture decisions so they are not re-litigated, but they are not the same as a feedback loop: they record what was decided, not what corrections during content production revealed about weaknesses in the standard definitions themselves.

The canonical system's feedback loop has a specific trigger: when a human makes a voice fix, structural rejection, or source resolution, that correction is classified by type, routed to the specific definition file that should have caught it, and applied. The plan's Phases 6–10 will produce human corrections at volume. Without the routing map and feedback log, those corrections will be applied locally (to the page) but not propagated to the standard (to prevent recurrence). After the first tab completes, the system will have learned nothing structurally — Phase 6 on the second tab starts from the same baseline as Phase 6 on the first tab.

---

## 4. One Structural Change

**Assign Phase 6.1 as a feedback log bootstrapping step, and tie the Tab Status Board's "Content Pass Open" gate to the routing map existing.**

Concretely: before Phase 6 runs on the first tab, a single build step produces the correction-to-file routing map (the design step in canonical Layer 6). This is not a large task — it requires listing the correction types the plan already knows will occur (voice, structure, information type, source conflict, component choice, naming, template gap) and mapping each to the target definition file and update format. The routing map is then the artifact that Phase 6 human reviewers reference when they make a correction: instead of fixing the page only, they log the correction to the map.

This single addition — add a "Feedback routing map exists" check to the Content Pass Open gate on the Tab Status Board — converts the system from open-loop to closed-loop without adding a new phase, without gating on the full Feedback System being built, and without slowing Phases 2–5. It means the first tab's content corrections become structural improvements to the definitions before the second tab runs. That is the canonical system's core compounding property, and the plan currently produces none of it.</result>

<summary>Agent "Product review: reader outcome perspective" completed</summary>
<result>I now have a complete picture of all three documents. Here is the product/reader outcome review.

---

## Reader Outcome Review

### 1. Reader Outcome Verdict

**Partial — with a specific failure mode that is structural, not incidental.**

The plan will likely produce technically sound pages — correct schema, verified claims, named headings, audience-scoped voice. What it may not produce is a docs site where a gateway operator, arriving cold, can find what they need without friction. The plan optimises heavily for per-page correctness and process compliance. Reader discoverability — can someone at the evaluation stage find the comparison they need? does the site surface the right starting point for a developer graduating from builder? — receives no explicit checkpoint. The audience design work (Phase 1) is sophisticated and JTBD-aware, but Phase 12 (cross-tab journeys) is deferred until all tabs are complete. This means readers arriving mid-build face a docs site where tabs are internally coherent but inter-tab handoffs (the graduation path from `builder → developer → gateway`) are not enforced until the end. For a reader at the gateway stage, the most critical content — "why self-host instead of using the hosted API?" — may exist in perfect prose but be unreachable without already knowing where to look.

---

### 2. Top 3 Reader Experience Risks

**Risk 1: Journey coherence gates exist per-section but not per-reader-path.**

The plan includes critical review of voice consistency and journey handoffs at the section level (Phase 6 critical review agent). But handoffs between tabs — where the actual reader journey crosses from About → Builders → Developers → Gateways — are not reviewed until Phase 12, which only runs after all 5 tabs are complete. A reader following the graduation path through partially-published content will hit dead ends, contradictory descriptions of the same concept (NaaP explained for builders vs developers vs gateway operators), and missing CTAs at the boundary where they should move between tabs. This would not be caught by any quality gate until the final phase.

**Risk 2: The page brief bottleneck creates a context collapse problem.**

Phase 6 requires human approval of every page brief before writing begins. A brief captures: audience, arriving question, single job, prev/next page. The system correctly prevents "write in a vacuum." But the brief format is defined at the page level, not at the journey level. A human approving 30 briefs for the Gateways tab is approving them in isolation — there is no tool or prompt in the plan that requires the reviewer to check whether brief N makes sense as a follow-on from brief N-1. The plan says a section-level review agent checks "prev → this → next handoffs" after drafts exist. By that point, pages have been written to mismatched assumptions, and the cost of revision is real. A reader would experience this as pages that are individually correct but do not feel like they belong together.

**Risk 3: The veracity gate protects factual accuracy but not reader trust calibration.**

The veracity engine (Phase 7) is designed to clear factual claims against authoritative sources. This is correct and necessary. What it does not check is whether the page correctly signals its own confidence level to the reader. A page about staking rewards that says "typical returns are X%" with a verified REVIEW marker that passes is still misleading if X% reflects a state that changes every 30 days and the page carries no staleness signal to the reader. The staleness matrix in Phase 7 defines re-verification windows for internal quality control — but there is no plan for how that staleness information is surfaced to a reader who arrives at a page that was verified six months ago. The `veracityStatus` frontmatter field is internal metadata, not reader-facing communication. No phase addresses this gap.

---

### 3. Missing Reader-First Gates

**Missing: A "cold arrival test" gate before any tab goes to production.**

No phase requires: "Can a reader with the correct audience profile for this tab, arriving from a plausible external source (search, referral, Discord link), find the page they need in under 3 navigation steps?" The Tab Status Board tracks process compliance (`IA Approved`, `Terminology Locked`, `Content Scan Done`) but has no gate that reflects whether the navigation experience for a real reader works. The portal + navigator pages (Phase 11) are built last — meaning during Phases 6–10 there is no enforced entry point test.

**Missing: A cross-persona conflict check on shared pages.**

Several tabs (About, Community, possibly Developers) contain pages that serve more than one audience. The plan handles multi-audience frontmatter as an open decision at Phase 0, but there is no gate that checks whether a shared page — say, a page about AI inference that serves both `builder/ai` and `developer/ai` personas — actually serves both or serves one well and confuses the other. The information type taxonomy and voice rules are audience-scoped. A page targeting two audiences simultaneously receives no structured review for whether it achieves coherence for both readers simultaneously.

**Missing: A first-page-in-the-journey review before the full section is written.**

The pilot scope for Gateways is the first section from the approved IA. Within that section, pages are written in parallel by multiple agents. There is no gate that requires the first page in the linear journey for a given persona to be reviewed and approved before writing begins on subsequent pages. Each page brief captures `prev/next`, but if the first page is wrong about what it hands off to the reader, every downstream page brief is anchored to a flawed assumption.

---

### 4. One Recommendation

**Add a reader journey smoke-test to the Phase 3 (IA Audit) human approval gate — before any content is written.**

The current IA Approved gate requires: tab-map exists + critical review of structure + human approves. Add one required item: for each primary persona defined in the audience design doc, trace the linear journey through the approved tab map and confirm that (a) the first page in the journey answers the persona's arriving question, (b) each page in the linear sequence explicitly hands off to the next, and (c) no required job-to-be-done is only served by on-demand pages. This takes roughly 20 minutes per persona per tab and requires no new tooling — only a structured checklist appended to the tab-map approval review. It catches journey coherence failures at the architecture stage, where fixing them costs a doc re-draft, not a content re-write. Without this, the plan can complete perfectly and still produce a tab where the gateway operator trying to understand whether they should graduate from hosted API to self-hosted cannot find their answer in the expected sequence.</result>
